structure_blocks.heading: Buyer's Guide Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement
heading_path: Buyer's Guide Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement
structure_blocks.text: Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement Canada’s Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement limits access to competitive contracts to Canadian suppliers and suppliers from countries that provide reciprocal access to Canadian suppliers through trade agreements, only when such trade agreements apply. Canadian suppliers remain eligible in all cases. Procedure for implementing the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement Under the Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement (referred to as the Policy herein after), a foreign supplier's eligibility will depend on whether the procurement solicitation is subject to a trade agreement between Canada and the supplier's jurisdiction. Buyers should follow the steps outlined below. Step 1: Determine trade agreement applicability As an initial and mandatory step, buyers must determine which trade agreements, if any, apply to a particular procurement. This helps you assess which suppliers are eligible for the procurement under the Policy. Note: This step must be completed in all cases, regardless of whether the Policy is ultimately applicable to the procurement. For additional guidance, buyers should refer to Trade agreement applicability and to GCpedia trade agreement guidance (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Step 2: Determine if the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement applies to the procurement process Next, buyers are to determine whether this Policy applies, in accordance with Sections 4.1 - 4.3 of the Policy: If the Policy does not apply, buyers can proceed directly to step 4 If the Policy applies, proceed to step 3 Canada reserves the right to apply to the requirements of the Policy to procurements that would normally be excluded. If a business owner requests to apply the Policy to an excluded procurement, then buyers are to proceed as if the Policy applies. Note : You must continue to comply with all obligations under any applicable trade agreement, regardless of whether the Policy applies. Step 3: Determine if an exception to the Policy applies Exceptions to the Policy must be applied on a case-by-case basis, and only where strictly necessary. Applying an exception expands competition for a specific procurement to a broader group of suppliers (i.e. Suppliers of non-trading partners) to ensure that Canada can acquire what it needs. However, applying an exception does not eliminate the requirement to solicit offers, nor does it override the applicability of trade agreements. The list of exceptions to the Policy can be found in the section 4.4 of the Policy. While buyers and business owners are expected to collaborate in conducting any necessary market analysis or pre-solicitation activities, the business owner is ultimately responsible for providing sufficient evidence to justify the use of an exception. If the business owner fails to provide sufficient evidence to the satisfaction of the buyer, an exception cannot be applied. An exception must be fully documented on the procurement file, including a clear description of the procurement strategy, the rationale for applying the exception, and any other supporting documents. As per Section 4.5 of the Policy, the “best value” and “public interest” exceptions must be approved in writing by an assistant deputy minister (ADM), or a person of equivalent rank, who is responsible for awarding the designated contract. This approval must be included in the procurement file. Step 4: Determine which suppliers are eligible under the Policy Buyers should next determine, based on the previous steps, which suppliers are eligible for a particular procurement: a. Determine which trade agreements apply to the procurement. Proceed to b. b. Is the procurement subject to the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement? If “no”, there are no restrictions on suppliers' eligibility under the Policy – Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner, and Suppliers of Non-Trading partners are all eligible. If “yes”, proceed to c. c. Do any exceptions to the Policy apply to the procurement? If “yes”, there are no restrictions on a supplier's eligibility under the Policy – Canadian suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner, and Suppliers of Non-Trading Partners are all eligible. If “no”, proceed to d. d. Do any international trade agreements apply to the procurement? (refer to Legislation and policies – Trade agreements – General principles of trade agreements for further details) If “no”, only Canadian Suppliers are eligible. If “yes”, only Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner are eligible. Step 5: Implementation in contractual documents For all new procurements, buyers must include the appropriate language and clauses based on which suppliers are eligible under the Policy. Request for Proposals (RFPs) To implement the Policy in RFP s in the tender notice, buyers are to: identify applicable trade agreements as per the usual process include one of the following in the description field of the tender notice: If the Policy applies : “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers (if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner).” If the Policy doesn’t apply: “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner, are eligible for this solicitation of offers." In the RFP buyers are to: include the appropriate reciprocal procurement clauses adjust as needed for the specific requirement Supply arrangements Existing supply arrangements are to be amended to include the reciprocal procurement clauses at the next renewal, which must be completed by July 14, 2026. Until the Supply Arrangement is renewed, solicitations issued under existing SA s must not include reciprocal procurement requirements. To implement the Policy, the Request for Supply Arrangement (RFSA) should indicate that suppliers from any jurisdiction may prequalify under the RFSA , but include an indication that competition may be limited for individual requirements under the SA . In the RFSA tender notice, buyers are to: identify applicable trade agreements include this statement in the Description field: “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner are eligible for this solicitation of offers. Eligibility for individual Requests for Proposals against the Supply Arrangement may be limited to Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner". In the RFSA buyers are to include the relevant reciprocal procurement clause. For individual solicitations under the supply arrangement, follow the RFP instructions above. Standing offers When renewing standing offers, include the reciprocal procurement clauses. Buyers have two options for how to apply the Policy in a Request for Standing Offers (RFSO): Option A : Limit eligibility to Canadian suppliers, and if any international trade agreements are applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. Use this approach when you expect all resulting contracts can be fulfilled by those groups (e.g., exceptions are not expected to be applied to call-ups). Option B : Allow suppliers of any jurisdiction to prequalify under the RFSO but give right of first refusal to Canadian suppliers and if applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. Use this when some contracts may need to be fulfilled by Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner (e.g. it is expected the exceptions will be applied to certain call-ups). In the RFSO tender notice, buyers are to: identify applicable trade agreements, as normal include one of these statements in the description field: If the Policy applies to the RFSO (Option A): “This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers, [if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner. ]” If the Policy applies to the RFSO via a right of first refusal (Option B) or if the Policy doesn’t apply: “Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner are eligible for this solicitation of offers. In the RFSO , buyers are then to: include reciprocal procurement clauses and adjust as appropriate if using Option B, formulate the call-up procedures to sort suppliers into a stream of Canadian Suppliers and, if applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. The stream of Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an applicable trading partner must have a right of first refusal on any call-ups. Suppliers of a non-trading partner can be awarded call-ups only where no Canadian Suppliers or Suppliers of an applicable trading partner can fulfill the requirements of the call-up, or where a reciprocal procurement exception applies. Step 6: Evaluation process For procurements subject to the Policy, suppliers must declare that they are either a Canadian supplier, or a Supplier of an applicable trading partner (if the procurement is subject to any international trade agreement(s)) This declaration is mandatory and assessed on pass/fail basis. Reciprocal Procurement declarations must be provided before contract award. Buyers may request the declaration at the time of offer closing, but they cannot make it a mandatory requirement at offer closing. If not submitted at offer closing, buyers must notify the supplier to submit it within two business days of the notification. Buyers should confirm the address provided by the supplier in their declaration is valid. If there are concerns about the accuracy of the declaration, buyers should follow the verification process outlined in Step 8. Step 7: Post-award Contractors must continue to comply with their declaration and the requirements of the Policy for the entire duration of the contract. If a buyer or business owner suspects that a contractor no longer meets these requirements, they are to follow the verification process outlined in Step 8. Under the Policy, contractors are required to notify the buyer of any change in location during the contract period. If the relocation results in non-compliance with the requirements of the Policy, the buyer should consult Legal Services to determine the appropriate response. This decision should take into account the operational impact, and any representations made by the contractor. Step 8: Verification process Suppliers must remain compliant with their declaration throughout the duration of the tendering period and the resulting contract. Suppliers are not required to provide evidence of eligibility at the time of offer submission; however, suppliers should have evidence of eligibility ready in case it is audited or requested. Canada reserves the right to verify supplier declarations at any time, including in response to credible complaints, as part of routine reviews, or following contract award. Buyers may request additional documentation to verify a supplier’s declaration. If an in-depth verification is initiated, buyers are to send a letter requesting the supplier provide supporting evidence within five business days using the template letter available for download: Letter A and Letter B (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). If the supplier is found to be compliant, no further action is required. If a supplier fails to provide the requested evidence or is found to have submitted false or misleading information, potential actions may include: Disqualifying the offer Declaring the supplier ineligible for contract award Retaining the bid deposit Termination the contract Setting aside the Standing Offer or Supply Arrangement and terminating any resulting call-ups/contracts Retaining holdbacks Disqualifying the supplier from future procurements under the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy Buyers must consult Legal Services before deciding on a remedy. This decision should take into account the operational impact and any representations made by the supplier. Template letters to inform suppliers whose offer is disqualified due to failing to meet the conditions for participation requirements set out the Policy are available for download Letter A and Letter B accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Enquiries Enquiries on the application of the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement should be directed to the Strategic Policy Sector's Trade Agreement Unit .

raw_text:
Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement Canada’s Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement limits access to competitive contracts to Canadian suppliers and suppliers from countries that provide reciprocal access to Canadian suppliers through trade agreements, only when such trade agreements apply. Canadian suppliers remain eligible in all cases. Procedure for implementing the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement Under the Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement (referred to as the Policy herein after), a foreign supplier's eligibility will depend on whether the procurement solicitation is subject to a trade agreement between Canada and the supplier's jurisdiction. Buyers should follow the steps outlined below. Step 1: Determine trade agreement applicability As an initial and mandatory step, buyers must determine which trade agreements, if any, apply to a particular procurement. This helps you assess which suppliers are eligible for the procurement under the Policy. Note: This step must be completed in all cases, regardless of whether the Policy is ultimately applicable to the procurement. For additional guidance, buyers should refer to Trade agreement applicability and to GCpedia trade agreement guidance (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Step 2: Determine if the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement applies to the procurement process Next, buyers are to determine whether this Policy applies, in accordance with Sections 4.1 - 4.3 of the Policy: If the Policy does not apply, buyers can proceed directly to step 4 If the Policy applies, proceed to step 3 Canada reserves the right to apply to the requirements of the Policy to procurements that would normally be excluded. If a business owner requests to apply the Policy to an excluded procurement, then buyers are to proceed as if the Policy applies. Note : You must continue to comply with all obligations under any applicable trade agreement, regardless of whether the Policy applies. Step 3: Determine if an exception to the Policy applies Exceptions to the Policy must be applied on a case-by-case basis, and only where strictly necessary. Applying an exception expands competition for a specific procurement to a broader group of suppliers (i.e. Suppliers of non-trading partners) to ensure that Canada can acquire what it needs. However, applying an exception does not eliminate the requirement to solicit offers, nor does it override the applicability of trade agreements. The list of exceptions to the Policy can be found in the section 4.4 of the Policy. While buyers and business owners are expected to collaborate in conducting any necessary market analysis or pre-solicitation activities, the business owner is ultimately responsible for providing sufficient evidence to justify the use of an exception. If the business owner fails to provide sufficient evidence to the satisfaction of the buyer, an exception cannot be applied. An exception must be fully documented on the procurement file, including a clear description of the procurement strategy, the rationale for applying the exception, and any other supporting documents. As per Section 4.5 of the Policy, the “best value” and “public interest” exceptions must be approved in writing by an assistant deputy minister (ADM), or a person of equivalent rank, who is responsible for awarding the designated contract. This approval must be included in the procurement file. Step 4: Determine which suppliers are eligible under the Policy Buyers should next determine, based on the previous steps, which suppliers are eligible for a particular procurement: a. Determine which trade agreements apply to the procurement. Proceed to b. b. Is the procurement subject to the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement? If “no”, there are no restrictions on suppliers' eligibility under the Policy – Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner, and Suppliers of Non-Trading partners are all eligible. If “yes”, proceed to c. c. Do any exceptions to the Policy apply to the procurement? If “yes”, there are no restrictions on a supplier's eligibility under the Policy – Canadian suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner, and Suppliers of Non-Trading Partners are all eligible. If “no”, proceed to d. d. Do any international trade agreements apply to the procurement? (refer to Legislation and policies – Trade agreements – General principles of trade agreements for further details) If “no”, only Canadian Suppliers are eligible. If “yes”, only Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner are eligible. Step 5: Implementation in contractual documents For all new procurements, buyers must include the appropriate language and clauses based on which suppliers are eligible under the Policy. Request for Proposals (RFPs) To implement the Policy in RFP s in the tender notice, buyers are to: identify applicable trade agreements as per the usual process include one of the following in the description field of the tender notice: If the Policy applies : “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers (if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner).” If the Policy doesn’t apply: “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner, are eligible for this solicitation of offers." In the RFP buyers are to: include the appropriate reciprocal procurement clauses adjust as needed for the specific requirement Supply arrangements Existing supply arrangements are to be amended to include the reciprocal procurement clauses at the next renewal, which must be completed by July 14, 2026. Until the Supply Arrangement is renewed, solicitations issued under existing SA s must not include reciprocal procurement requirements. To implement the Policy, the Request for Supply Arrangement (RFSA) should indicate that suppliers from any jurisdiction may prequalify under the RFSA , but include an indication that competition may be limited for individual requirements under the SA . In the RFSA tender notice, buyers are to: identify applicable trade agreements include this statement in the Description field: “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner are eligible for this solicitation of offers. Eligibility for individual Requests for Proposals against the Supply Arrangement may be limited to Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner". In the RFSA buyers are to include the relevant reciprocal procurement clause. For individual solicitations under the supply arrangement, follow the RFP instructions above. Standing offers When renewing standing offers, include the reciprocal procurement clauses. Buyers have two options for how to apply the Policy in a Request for Standing Offers (RFSO): Option A : Limit eligibility to Canadian suppliers, and if any international trade agreements are applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. Use this approach when you expect all resulting contracts can be fulfilled by those groups (e.g., exceptions are not expected to be applied to call-ups). Option B : Allow suppliers of any jurisdiction to prequalify under the RFSO but give right of first refusal to Canadian suppliers and if applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. Use this when some contracts may need to be fulfilled by Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner (e.g. it is expected the exceptions will be applied to certain call-ups). In the RFSO tender notice, buyers are to: identify applicable trade agreements, as normal include one of these statements in the description field: If the Policy applies to the RFSO (Option A): “This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers, [if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner. ]” If the Policy applies to the RFSO via a right of first refusal (Option B) or if the Policy doesn’t apply: “Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner are eligible for this solicitation of offers. In the RFSO , buyers are then to: include reciprocal procurement clauses and adjust as appropriate if using Option B, formulate the call-up procedures to sort suppliers into a stream of Canadian Suppliers and, if applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. The stream of Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an applicable trading partner must have a right of first refusal on any call-ups. Suppliers of a non-trading partner can be awarded call-ups only where no Canadian Suppliers or Suppliers of an applicable trading partner can fulfill the requirements of the call-up, or where a reciprocal procurement exception applies. Step 6: Evaluation process For procurements subject to the Policy, suppliers must declare that they are either a Canadian supplier, or a Supplier of an applicable trading partner (if the procurement is subject to any international trade agreement(s)) This declaration is mandatory and assessed on pass/fail basis. Reciprocal Procurement declarations must be provided before contract award. Buyers may request the declaration at the time of offer closing, but they cannot make it a mandatory requirement at offer closing. If not submitted at offer closing, buyers must notify the supplier to submit it within two business days of the notification. Buyers should confirm the address provided by the supplier in their declaration is valid. If there are concerns about the accuracy of the declaration, buyers should follow the verification process outlined in Step 8. Step 7: Post-award Contractors must continue to comply with their declaration and the requirements of the Policy for the entire duration of the contract. If a buyer or business owner suspects that a contractor no longer meets these requirements, they are to follow the verification process outlined in Step 8. Under the Policy, contractors are required to notify the buyer of any change in location during the contract period. If the relocation results in non-compliance with the requirements of the Policy, the buyer should consult Legal Services to determine the appropriate response. This decision should take into account the operational impact, and any representations made by the contractor. Step 8: Verification process Suppliers must remain compliant with their declaration throughout the duration of the tendering period and the resulting contract. Suppliers are not required to provide evidence of eligibility at the time of offer submission; however, suppliers should have evidence of eligibility ready in case it is audited or requested. Canada reserves the right to verify supplier declarations at any time, including in response to credible complaints, as part of routine reviews, or following contract award. Buyers may request additional documentation to verify a supplier’s declaration. If an in-depth verification is initiated, buyers are to send a letter requesting the supplier provide supporting evidence within five business days using the template letter available for download: Letter A and Letter B (accessible only on the Government of Canada network). If the supplier is found to be compliant, no further action is required. If a supplier fails to provide the requested evidence or is found to have submitted false or misleading information, potential actions may include: Disqualifying the offer Declaring the supplier ineligible for contract award Retaining the bid deposit Termination the contract Setting aside the Standing Offer or Supply Arrangement and terminating any resulting call-ups/contracts Retaining holdbacks Disqualifying the supplier from future procurements under the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy Buyers must consult Legal Services before deciding on a remedy. This decision should take into account the operational impact and any representations made by the supplier. Template letters to inform suppliers whose offer is disqualified due to failing to meet the conditions for participation requirements set out the Policy are available for download Letter A and Letter B accessible only on the Government of Canada network). Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement

Canada’s Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement limits access to competitive contracts to Canadian suppliers and suppliers from countries that provide reciprocal access to Canadian suppliers through trade agreements, only when such trade agreements apply. Canadian suppliers remain eligible in all cases.

Procedure for implementing the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement

Under the Interim Policy on Reciprocal Procurement (referred to as the Policy herein after), a foreign supplier's eligibility will depend on whether the procurement solicitation is subject to a trade agreement between Canada and the supplier's jurisdiction.

Buyers should follow the steps outlined below.

Step 1: Determine trade agreement applicability

As an initial and mandatory step, buyers must determine which trade agreements, if any, apply to a particular procurement. This helps you assess which suppliers are eligible for the procurement under the Policy.

Note: This step must be completed in all cases, regardless of whether the Policy is ultimately applicable to the procurement.

For additional guidance, buyers should refer to Trade agreement applicability and to GCpedia trade agreement guidance (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Step 2: Determine if the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement applies to the procurement process

Next, buyers are to determine whether this Policy applies, in accordance with Sections 4.1 - 4.3 of the Policy:

If the Policy does not apply, buyers can proceed directly to step 4

If the Policy applies, proceed to step 3

Canada reserves the right to apply to the requirements of the Policy to procurements that would normally be excluded. If a business owner requests to apply the Policy to an excluded procurement, then buyers are to proceed as if the Policy applies.

Note : You must continue to comply with all obligations under any applicable trade agreement, regardless of whether the Policy applies.

Step 3: Determine if an exception to the Policy applies

Exceptions to the Policy must be applied on a case-by-case basis, and only where strictly necessary.

Applying an exception expands competition for a specific procurement to a broader group of suppliers (i.e. Suppliers of non-trading partners) to ensure that Canada can acquire what it needs. However, applying an exception does not eliminate the requirement to solicit offers, nor does it override the applicability of trade agreements.

The list of exceptions to the Policy can be found in the section 4.4 of the Policy.

While buyers and business owners are expected to collaborate in conducting any necessary market analysis or pre-solicitation activities, the business owner is ultimately responsible for providing sufficient evidence to justify the use of an exception. If the business owner fails to provide sufficient evidence to the satisfaction of the buyer, an exception cannot be applied.

An exception must be fully documented on the procurement file, including a clear description of the procurement strategy, the rationale for applying the exception, and any other supporting documents.

As per Section 4.5 of the Policy, the “best value” and “public interest” exceptions must be approved in writing by an assistant deputy minister (ADM), or a person of equivalent rank, who is responsible for awarding the designated contract. This approval must be included in the procurement file.

Step 4: Determine which suppliers are eligible under the Policy

Buyers should next determine, based on the previous steps, which suppliers are eligible for a particular procurement:

a. Determine which trade agreements apply to the procurement. Proceed to b. b. Is the procurement subject to the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement? If “no”, there are no restrictions on suppliers' eligibility under the Policy – Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner, and Suppliers of Non-Trading partners are all eligible. If “yes”, proceed to c. c. Do any exceptions to the Policy apply to the procurement? If “yes”, there are no restrictions on a supplier's eligibility under the Policy – Canadian suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner, and Suppliers of Non-Trading Partners are all eligible. If “no”, proceed to d. d. Do any international trade agreements apply to the procurement? (refer to Legislation and policies – Trade agreements – General principles of trade agreements for further details) If “no”, only Canadian Suppliers are eligible. If “yes”, only Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner are eligible.

Step 5: Implementation in contractual documents

For all new procurements, buyers must include the appropriate language and clauses based on which suppliers are eligible under the Policy.

To implement the Policy in RFP s in the tender notice, buyers are to:

identify applicable trade agreements as per the usual process

include one of the following in the description field of the tender notice: If the Policy applies : “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers (if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner).” If the Policy doesn’t apply: “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner, are eligible for this solicitation of offers."

If the Policy applies : “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers (if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner).”

If the Policy doesn’t apply: “Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner, are eligible for this solicitation of offers."

In the RFP buyers are to:

include the appropriate reciprocal procurement clauses

adjust as needed for the specific requirement

Supply arrangements

Existing supply arrangements are to be amended to include the reciprocal procurement clauses at the next renewal, which must be completed by July 14, 2026. Until the Supply Arrangement is renewed, solicitations issued under existing SA s must not include reciprocal procurement requirements.

To implement the Policy, the Request for Supply Arrangement (RFSA) should indicate that suppliers from any jurisdiction may prequalify under the RFSA , but include an indication that competition may be limited for individual requirements under the SA .

In the RFSA tender notice, buyers are to:

identify applicable trade agreements

include this statement in the Description field:

“Policy on Reciprocal Procurement: Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner are eligible for this solicitation of offers. Eligibility for individual Requests for Proposals against the Supply Arrangement may be limited to Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner".

In the RFSA buyers are to include the relevant reciprocal procurement clause.

For individual solicitations under the supply arrangement, follow the RFP instructions above.

Standing offers

When renewing standing offers, include the reciprocal procurement clauses. Buyers have two options for how to apply the Policy in a Request for Standing Offers (RFSO):

Option A : Limit eligibility to Canadian suppliers, and if any international trade agreements are applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. Use this approach when you expect all resulting contracts can be fulfilled by those groups (e.g., exceptions are not expected to be applied to call-ups).

Option B : Allow suppliers of any jurisdiction to prequalify under the RFSO but give right of first refusal to Canadian suppliers and if applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. Use this when some contracts may need to be fulfilled by Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner (e.g. it is expected the exceptions will be applied to certain call-ups).

In the RFSO tender notice, buyers are to:

identify applicable trade agreements, as normal

include one of these statements in the description field: If the Policy applies to the RFSO (Option A): “This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers, [if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner. ]” If the Policy applies to the RFSO via a right of first refusal (Option B) or if the Policy doesn’t apply: “Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner are eligible for this solicitation of offers.

If the Policy applies to the RFSO (Option A): “This solicitation of offers is open only to Canadian Suppliers, [if subject to any international trade agreement(s): and to Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner. ]”

If the Policy applies to the RFSO via a right of first refusal (Option B) or if the Policy doesn’t apply: “Barring any other conditions or international sanctions, Canadian Suppliers, Suppliers of an Applicable Trading Partner and Suppliers of a Non-Trading Partner are eligible for this solicitation of offers.

In the RFSO , buyers are then to:

include reciprocal procurement clauses and adjust as appropriate

if using Option B, formulate the call-up procedures to sort suppliers into a stream of Canadian Suppliers and, if applicable, Suppliers of an applicable trading partner. The stream of Canadian Suppliers and Suppliers of an applicable trading partner must have a right of first refusal on any call-ups. Suppliers of a non-trading partner can be awarded call-ups only where no Canadian Suppliers or Suppliers of an applicable trading partner can fulfill the requirements of the call-up, or where a reciprocal procurement exception applies.

Step 6: Evaluation process

For procurements subject to the Policy, suppliers must declare that they are either

a Canadian supplier, or

a Supplier of an applicable trading partner (if the procurement is subject to any international trade agreement(s))

This declaration is mandatory and assessed on pass/fail basis.

Reciprocal Procurement declarations must be provided before contract award. Buyers may request the declaration at the time of offer closing, but they cannot make it a mandatory requirement at offer closing. If not submitted at offer closing, buyers must notify the supplier to submit it within two business days of the notification.

Buyers should confirm the address provided by the supplier in their declaration is valid. If there are concerns about the accuracy of the declaration, buyers should follow the verification process outlined in Step 8.

Step 7: Post-award

Contractors must continue to comply with their declaration and the requirements of the Policy for the entire duration of the contract.

If a buyer or business owner suspects that a contractor no longer meets these requirements, they are to follow the verification process outlined in Step 8.

Under the Policy, contractors are required to notify the buyer of any change in location during the contract period. If the relocation results in non-compliance with the requirements of the Policy, the buyer should consult Legal Services to determine the appropriate response. This decision should take into account the operational impact, and any representations made by the contractor.

Step 8: Verification process

Suppliers must remain compliant with their declaration throughout the duration of the tendering period and the resulting contract. Suppliers are not required to provide evidence of eligibility at the time of offer submission; however, suppliers should have evidence of eligibility ready in case it is audited or requested.

Canada reserves the right to verify supplier declarations at any time, including in response to credible complaints, as part of routine reviews, or following contract award. Buyers may request additional documentation to verify a supplier’s declaration.

If an in-depth verification is initiated, buyers are to send a letter requesting the supplier provide supporting evidence within five business days using the template letter available for download: Letter A and Letter B (accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

If the supplier is found to be compliant, no further action is required.

If a supplier fails to provide the requested evidence or is found to have submitted false or misleading information, potential actions may include:

Disqualifying the offer

Declaring the supplier ineligible for contract award

Retaining the bid deposit

Termination the contract

Setting aside the Standing Offer or Supply Arrangement and terminating any resulting call-ups/contracts

Retaining holdbacks

Disqualifying the supplier from future procurements under the Ineligibility and Suspension Policy

Buyers must consult Legal Services before deciding on a remedy. This decision should take into account the operational impact and any representations made by the supplier. Template letters to inform suppliers whose offer is disqualified due to failing to meet the conditions for participation requirements set out the Policy are available for download Letter A and Letter B accessible only on the Government of Canada network).

Enquiries

Enquiries on the application of the Policy on Reciprocal Procurement should be directed to the Strategic Policy Sector's Trade Agreement Unit .
